meta.title: Notification of changes in taxonomic opinion previously published outside the IJSEM
meta.journalTitle: International Journal of Systematic and Evolutionary Microbiology

abstractHtml: The Bacteriological Code deals with the nomenclature of prokaryotes. This may include existing names (the Approved Lists of Bacterial Names) as well as new names and new combinations. In this sense the Code is also dealing indirectly with taxonomic opinions. However, as with most codes of nomenclature there are no mechanisms for formally recording taxonomic opinions that do not involve the creation of new names or new combinations. In particular, it would be desirable for taxonomic opinions resulting from the creation of synonyms or emended descriptions to be made widely available to the public. In 2004, the Editorial Board of the International Journal of Systematic and Evolutionary Microbiology (IJSEM) agreed unanimously that it was desirable to cover such changes in taxonomic opinions (i.e. the creation of synonyms or the emendation of circumscriptions) previously published outside the IJSEM, and to introduce a List of Changes in Taxonomic Opinion [Notification of changes in taxonomic opinion previously published outside the IJSEM; Euzéby et al. (2004). Int J Syst Evol Microbiol 54, 14291430]. Scientists wishing to have changes in taxonomic opinion included in future lists should send one copy of the pertinent reprint or a photocopy or a PDF file thereof to the IJSEM Editorial Office or to the Lists Editor. It must be stressed that the date of proposed taxonomic changes is the date of the original publication not the date of publication of the list. Taxonomic opinions included in the List of Changes in Taxonomic Opinion cannot be considered as validly published nor, in any other way, approved by the International Committee on Systematics of Prokaryotes and its Judicial Commission. The names that are to be used are those that are the correct names (in the sense of Principle 6) in the opinion of the bacteriologist, with a given circumscription, position and rank. A particular name, circumscription, position and rank does not have to be adopted in all circumstances. Consequently, the List of Changes in Taxonomic Opinion must be considered as a service to bacteriology and it has no official character, other than providing a centralized point for registering/indexing such changes in a way that makes them easily accessible to the scientific community.
summaryHtml:
This article announces the establishment of a formal mechanism for recording and disseminating taxonomic opinions that do not involve creating new names or combinations. The International Journal of Systematic and Evolutionary Microbiology (IJSEM) introduced a 'List of Changes in Taxonomic Opinion' to document previously published taxonomic revisions such as synonym designations and emended descriptions. This service addresses a gap in the Bacteriological Code, which lacks formal procedures for recording such changes. The list is indexed centrally, making taxonomic updates accessible to the scientific community. Importantly, the list has no official status and does not constitute valid publication or approval by the International Committee on Systematics of Prokaryotes. Scientists can submit relevant reprints or PDFs to the IJSEM Editorial Office for inclusion. The article provides the first such list, documenting numerous bacterial taxa including emended descriptions of species like Arthrobacter cumminsii and Bacillus coagulans, new synonymies such as Corynebacterium nigricans being merged with Corynebacterium aurimucosum, and reclassifications of Falcivibrio species into the genus Mobiluncus.

Key findings

  • IJSEM established a formal registry for taxonomic opinions involving synonymies and emended descriptions previously published outside the journal
  • The List of Changes has no official status and does not represent valid publication or approval by systematic committees
  • The date of taxonomic changes is the original publication date, not the registry publication date
  • The first list includes multiple bacterial taxonomic changes including species emendations and new synonym designations
  • This service provides centralized indexing to increase accessibility of taxonomic updates to the scientific community
